He waited until there was a pause and then said to Petra: "Ren submitted calibration authorization records to the registry this morning. The documents have been classified for thirty years."

Petra looked at him. She said: "Did you know he was going to do this?"

"No."

She considered this for a moment. "Are they in the archive now?"

"Yes. In thirty-four nodes."

She nodded once. That was what she had needed to know.

The room continued doing what it had been doing. The relay coordinator finished her maintenance log. The implementation accord's third clause was resolved. The terminal reconnected. Corin set his coordination terminal aside and picked up the eastern community's documentation request, which had arrived three days ago and had not been fully processed yet, and worked on that instead.

Ren's entry sat in the registry in thirty-four places. No one in the warehouse had anything further to say about it. The record held it. That was what it did.

---

The courier arrived at 1113.

She came to the side door, which was still propped open, and looked into the room and said: "Is this the coalition coordination center?"

Corin looked at Petra and Dael. Petra said, "That's us."

The courier was a young woman who had been traveling for nine days and looked like it — dusty from the road and alert in the way that people become alert when they are tired and have been responsible for something specific for a long time. She was carrying a sealed document case. She checked Corin's name against a list she had in her breast pocket before she spoke to him.

"I have a document from the Timelost Community Collective of the eastern waystation settlements," she said. "For the coordinator of the distributed network and witness registry project. Addressed to whoever holds that role."

"That's me," Corin said.

She handed him the sealed case. The seal was wax, not institutional — the kind of personal seal that Timelost communities had used for formal correspondence for seventy years, before there were electronic authentication options and after, because the wax seal meant something specific to them that the electronic authentication did not.

He broke it and opened the case.

Inside was a single document, four pages, notarized. A formal request from the Timelost Community Collective of the eastern waystation settlements to join the distributed network and contribute their parallel records to the shared witness registry. The document was precisely structured — legal language, witness attestations, a careful accounting of what records they maintained, what format they used, and how long they had been maintaining them. They had been maintaining them since vol-008, before the network existed. They had been keeping records for years in the hope that there would eventually be something to join.

He read the completion date.

Three months ago.

"You've been waiting," he said.

"We wanted to be sure you'd built something to join," she said.

He read that again. Not the words she had just said — the words were simple enough. The fact behind them: that they had completed this document three months ago, when the distributed network did not exist, when the coalition was still not certain it would exist, when the registry was still in single-point custody and Theron Gale's upload was eleven days away from being received. They had made the decision to join before there was something to join. They had drafted the document and sealed it and sent a courier and waited.

"Will you stay tonight?" Petra asked the courier. "We can arrange a room."

"Thank you," the courier said. "One night."

---

Corin stayed in the warehouse after the others left to arrange the courier's accommodation. He did not need to stay — the chairs needed to go back to the stack and the table back to the wall, and neither task required him specifically. He did both slowly, not thinking about them.

The document was on the table while he worked.

He was not certain how to hold the fact of it. The network the document was requesting to join was eleven hours old in the form it was now — thirty-four distributed nodes, the synchronization protocol, the extended registry format that now included the *ceased* category and its seven sub-categories and the *witnessed absence* classification and the *After* field that Theron Gale had written himself in past tense and third person, already knowing. The network had been being built toward this form for three years. The document in front of him was three months old. They had been writing it while he was still in the middle of building.

Theron Gale's entry was in thirty-four nodes. It had been three days since the medical registry entry arrived. The status field read *completed — named.* He had named 847 people and then himself. He had timed the upload and the timing had been, in the end, exact.

The voice that had been half-annotated at 0341 on a morning several weeks ago was in the registry's *ceased* category, which had not existed the night before Iris created it. The bread recipe was in the registry. The street where Elara Venn had grown up was in the registry. They were not recovered. They were not restorable. They were recorded as gone, with everything the record could hold about what had been there before the loss. Three weeks of people reading those entries and adding annotations — family members, community contacts, people who had known the things that were now gone and wanted to name that knowing. The entries had grown. Not toward recovery. Toward being held.

He put the table back against the wall.

He picked up the eastern community's document and put it in his bag.

The warehouse was empty at 1156. It had held fourteen people for four hours, none of whom had been told to come. All of them had come anyway. The commitment they had made was not a vote and not a command and not a coalition in the structural sense — it was a set of independent choices that had arrived in the same room and produced the same outcome. The same network. The same registry. The same ongoing problem of keeping it running, which none of the choices solved and all of the choices made possible.

The document was three months old. The network it was joining was eleven hours old. The record that held Theron Gale's name — under both his names, in thirty-four places, not erasable in any single operation — was three days old. The voices and recipes and streets that had been lost and were still being read in the *ceased* registry were weeks old now, and still accumulating annotations, and would be read by whoever needed to read them for as long as the registry held.

None of it was finished. That was not an interim state on the way to being finished.

He walked out the side door and left it unlocked for the courier.

The morning continued in the Juncture district. The transit hub was running its regular schedule. A bread cart passed the warehouse on the street outside, three minutes after Corin had left, going toward the market. The driver did not know anyone had been in the warehouse. The Juncture district did not notice when the coalition committed to the preservation of what remained, because the Juncture district had other things to do, and commitment that was real did not require notice.

The door held the unlocked latch.

The document was in Corin's bag.

The network was in thirty-four places.

The morning was still going.
